title: Slow-cooker chocolate self-saucing pudding
subtitle: Batter and sauce cook together into one pudding
excerpt: A self-saucing chocolate pudding made in the slow cooker. The batter is spread in the pot, the sauce ingredients are scattered on top, boiling water is poured over without stirring, and two to three hours on high produces a sponge sitting over a pool of hot chocolate sauce. The sauce forms itself during the cook.
sourceNotes: Self-saucing puddings (also called magic puddings) became popular in Britain and Australia from the mid-twentieth century. The chemistry is reliable: the batter floats on the sugar-water layer, which sinks below and becomes the sauce while the batter cooks into a sponge. The slow cooker version works because the gentle, even heat prevents the sauce from over-reducing or burning.

content.text: Do not stir after adding the boiling water. The three-layer arrangement (batter at the bottom, dry sugar and cocoa scattered on top, boiling water poured over) looks alarming and makes no sense until you see the result. Stirring collapses the layers and produces a dense, heavy cake without sauce.

Line the slow cooker with baking parchment, leaving enough to overhang the sides. In a bowl, whisk together the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and 3 tablespoons {{cocoa-powder}}. In a jug, whisk the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{eggs}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and mix until smooth. Spread the batter evenly into the lined slow cooker pot. In a small bowl, combine the {{dark-brown-sugar}} and 2 tablespoons {{cocoa-powder}}. Scatter this mixture evenly over the batter, do not press it in. Slowly pour 350 ml boiling water over the top. Do not stir. Fit the lid and cook on high for 2 to 2.5 hours, until the sponge is set and pulling away from the sides. A skewer inserted into the sponge (not the sauce beneath) should come out clean. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: The sauce should be visible around the edges of the sponge. Use exactly 350 ml boiling water. Reduce cooking time by 15 minutes next time, the sauce disappears if the pudding is overcooked
items.cause: Too little water or cooked too long
items.symptom: Sponge is set but there is no sauce underneath

items.fix: Continue for another 30 minutes with the lid on. The edges will be done first, the centre takes longer in a slow cooker than in an oven
items.cause: Slow cooker runs cool, or batter too thick
items.symptom: Sponge still liquid in the centre after 2.5 hours

items.fix: Use exactly 150 g dark brown sugar in the sauce layer. The sugar concentrates as the water is absorbed upward into the batter during cooking
items.cause: Not enough dark brown sugar in the sauce layer, or too much water added

content.text: Chocolate self-saucing pudding is the most dramatic slow-cooker dessert. It looks impossible until the moment you spoon through the sponge and find the sauce pooled underneath. Serve immediately with vanilla ice cream or pouring cream. The sauce is absorbed quickly so there is no value in letting it sit.
